data.title: Can You Retire On $500,000?
data.description:
Introduction:

First of all, we want to say that everyone should save and invest as much as possible and set retirement savings goals high enough, even if they may seem out of reach. If the goalpost is set high enough, the investor will likely strive and find new ways to reach those goals. All that said, such goals are highly individualistic, based on personal needs and circumstances, and cannot be generalized. For most middle-class folks, we would recommend setting a minimum goal of $1 million, but preferably $1.5 million. When it comes to retirement savings, often more is better.

So, what if you cannot save $1 million? You may be too late in starting a retirement account, or you did not save enough. Maybe you saved over the years, but circumstances forced you to withdraw from them, and now it is not nearly enough. Or maybe retirement always looked too distant in the future. Whatever the case may be, there is always hope, and one need not panic; all you need is the right perspective of the situation and proper planning for the future.

Let’s say you never saved much for retirement. You are already in your mid-50s, and you only have $100,000 in your retirement accounts. Also, your income is modest, and you think you can only work for another 10-12 years. In such a scenario, can you start now and save $500,000 by the time you retire at 65? Assuming you are starting from a nominal savings of $100,000, to get to $500,000, you probably need to save $20,000 to $25,000 each year, and the rest can come from compounding. More importantly, can $500,000 suffice for 30 years of retirement and afford you a middle-class living standard? Sure, it was not long ago when $500,000 was still considered a sizable amount to retire with a reasonably comfortable middle-class living. But the recent bout of inflation has changed much of that. Now, it may be very difficult to manage retirement with only $500,000; however, it is not impossible, and you have options. You may have to be innovative and be flexible enough in terms of where you retire and how you spend your money in retirement.

Let’s assume you saved $500,000 in your retirement accounts by the time you retire at 65. We will also assume that you own a home now worth $400,000 (acquired at a much lower price 15 years ago, prior to retirement), but you still have a mortgage balance of $150,000. So, you have equity in the house worth about $250,000. However, this amount is not your retirement capital, as you still need a place to live.

Here are the major decisions our retiree will have to make:

  1. The first major decision you will have to make is to move to a much cheaper place (even if you have to move out of state). You may even have to downsize, which will also help in reducing other living expenses like utility bills and property taxes. You should find a home that costs you less than the equity in your current house so that the new place is fully paid for with no mortgage to worry about. You should also choose a state where both the state taxes and property taxes are low.
  2. The second most important decision you would make is when to start drawing Social Security. Assuming our retiree couple is 65, we would recommend waiting until the full retirement age of 66 years and 10 months (or 67 to round it off). We will also recommend not withdrawing too much from your retirement capital for the intervening two years. Instead, our retiree should look for part-time work or an online gig to make up for the lack of social security income. Let’s assume your social security benefits (for the couple) at 67 are $1,800 per person, and for the couple is $3,600.
  3. At 65, you are eligible for Medicare benefits, so healthcare expenses will be reasonable. But, with Medicare premiums and supplementary insurance, let’s assume you will have an expense of $500 a month for the couple.
  4. We will also assume that in retirement, you keep only one car, which is fully paid. With ride-share options available these days, most folks (in retirement) can manage with one car.
  5. You choose to avoid having expensive hobbies. Let’s say you spend no more than $500 a month (average) on hobbies and travel.

Now, we can see that our retiree couple needs roughly $17,000 on a yearly basis from their investment capital or an income yield of 3.5% from their investments. To account for some unexpected exigencies, we will assume that they should shoot for a 4% yield or $20,000 income on an annual basis. If they can save something out of this amount, they can always reinvest.

Also, a point to note here. For the first two years, since they are delaying the social security payments until 67, they should work part-time and fill the income gap. However, there may be a need for additional income during these two years, thereby requiring a higher yield (just the first two years).

Investment Portfolio for Retirement with $500,000:

Here we will describe in detail and recommend a portfolio that should generate between 5% and 6% income and nearly market-matching growth over a long-term basis. Sure, if you only need 4% income, as in our above example, you can reinvest the excess of income (which may be a good problem to have).

We will suggest two buckets inside your investment portfolio. Also, to keep things simple enough, we will go for a fund-based buy-and-hold portfolio.

Bucket-1: Income Bucket – 70% ($350,000)

This will be an income-focused bucket that will also provide roughly 7% income.

This bucket is a mix of ETFs and CEFs. Also, it has funds with high yields as well as moderate yields. The first two funds, SCHD and ADX, hold large-cap core blue-chip stocks. ADX, being a CEF, provides managed distributions at roughly 8% of its NAV on an annual basis, while SCHD provides distributions based on what it earns in dividend income. SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs and has a solid record of dividend growth. On the other hand, ADX has an excellent record of providing consistent income and excellent total returns over the years, with a history of nearly 95 years.

There are four other funds, each with a 16.5% allocation. They provide income from other diversified sources, such as bonds (FAGIX), real estate, REITs (RQI), utilities/infrastructure (UTG), and BDCs - Business Development Cos (BIZD).

The Combined Portfolio:

Dividend/Income from Bucket-1 = $24,505.

Dividend/Income from Bucket-2 = $4,260.

Total Income (annual) = $28,765.

Overall average yield on a $500,000 portfolio = 5.75% (100 * 28,765 / 500,000).

This portfolio generated more income than what our retiree couple needed, so they could reinvest the excess and grow the portfolio.

Risks with the Strategy:

As is true with any investment strategy, there are certain risks to the portfolio strategy suggested in this article. The biggest risk with this buy-and-hold two-bucket system is the market risk. If the overall market were to enter into a correction, the portfolio would also have losses. However, more than likely, the income should be protected. Moreover, there is “sequencing risk,” meaning what if the market were to take a deep dive just after you invest? The negative impacts of sequencing risk are potentially highest around the time of retirement. To mitigate both the above risks, one should invest in this strategy in many small installments rather than one lump sum. One can also utilize the dollar-cost-average method to invest in a new strategy, preferably 2-3 years (or more) prior to retirement.

Concluding Thoughts:

We are not suggesting that $500,000 is an appropriate amount or enough savings for everyone. In fact, one should aim for much higher. That being said, one size does not fit all. There are folks for whom it may still be a decent amount that, if properly invested, can serve them meaningfully. The portfolio described in the article can provide enough income along with Social Security, which should suffice for a middle-class living in many parts of the U.S. We have provided a hypothetical case study with a potential portfolio with $500,000 capital that can provide enough income and market-matched capital growth over the long term, and all that with a relatively low risk.
